title: Precision Psychiatry with Ronald Kessler, PhD - YouTube
text:
welcome to NYU langon insights on
Psychiatry a clinician's guide to the
latest psychiatric research I'm Dr theia
Gallagher each episode I interview a
leading psychiatric researcher about how
their work is shaping clinical practice
today it's my pleasure to welcome Dr
Ronald Kesler professor of healthc care
policy at Harvard Medical School Dr
Kesler is a giant in the field of
medicine his groundbreaking work on the
social determinance of mental health has
made him the most widely cited
researcher in all of psychiatry
and now he's leading efforts to take
Psychiatry into the era of precision
medicine in our conversation we talked
about why Precision Psychiatry has been
relatively slow to arrive what it will
mean for clinicians and how he's using
machine learning to identify atrisk
patients and craft individualized
treatment
plans well thank you so much Dr Kesler
for being with us today my pleasure can
you give us an overview of your current
research interests especially as they
relate to Precision Psychiatry yeah I'm
a psychiatric
epidemiologist and for many years my
work was purely Community descriptive
epidemiology estimating the prevalence
and carth of mental disorders and I did
look at treatment that you know how many
people got treatment and barriers to
treatment and stuff like that but I
never did clinical epidemiological stuff
and clinical epidemiological stuff is uh
by and large looking at what are the
predictors of whether treatment works
and that's kind of is uh nowadays called
Precision Psychiatry because there are a
lot of fancy statistical methods to to
look at that and in the past decade or
so I've gotten involved in doing more of
this clinical epidemiological stuff
initially through my involvement in
something called the Army Stars study
which was U which is an
ongoing very large initiative with many
different studies inside of it of
suicide among people in the US Military
and uh we started in I guess
2011 uh a few years after the rise in
the suicide rate among military people
was first observed and we're asked by
the department of army to come in and
see if we could have an Outsiders
perspective to see what's going on and
what could be done and so typically
psychiatric epidemiology never ask those
kind of questions you know we're usually
sort of on the outside saying hey look
mental disorders is a big problem
somebody should do something about that
somay but nobody really comes to ask us
what to do so this was sort of a a
unique actually really unique
opportunity unique in the in the sense
that the uh the military is a big
organization and it has an
unbelievable amount of data you know in
stuff I've done subsequently and and
where I spend most of my time now
working in commercial health plans and
in uh the Veterans Administration we
have medical and Pharmacy claims data
and procedure codes and test results and
sometimes we bring in data about the
neighborhood where the person lives so
you can look at Social determin but in
the active military we have IQ tests
personality tests blood samples
performance ratings criminal justice
data because the military run their own
police force we have child and
protective services data because the
military runs its own social welfare I
mean we just have this unbelievable
wealth of information I I for many years
found myself being very jealous of
people in Scandinavia because they had
all these registry data but actually now
in working in the active duty military I
have much more than they have in those
Registries because I said we have like
say IQ when somebody joins the military
they get an IQ Q test they also get
blood samples taken so we have access to
that we have personality scales because
the military uses personality test to
help figure out what's the right you
know the right occupation for the person
they Comm in so we started looking there
at risk factors for uh suicide and
that's really the first part of
precision medicine trying to figure out
who's at risk of something who should
you you know who's going to have the
heart attack that's the person we should
be going and doing the intervention with
and then subsequently the real core of
precision medicine is when we have
treatments that exist or better yet
multiple treatments that exist as we do
in
Psychiatry not just to predict who's at
highest risk but who
is highest benefit
uh among the people who get a treatment
who's the person that this treatment's
most likely to help and then if we do
that in parallel for multiple treatments
we can splice that data together to use
a technical term and say which of these
treatment Alternatives is the best one
for person number one and that could be
very different from the situation for
person number two but it all starts in
developing these risk models for how
well will this particular treatment work
for this particular person and then we
do that in parallel for many people and
many treatments and can fold it all
together to try to come up with a an
optimization scheme for how to get the
right treatment to the right person at
the right time okay so it sounds like
this this method or these methods are
possible when you have all these data
points um which like you're saying we
happen to have in the military so is it
possible to do this work with the
general population or are we going to
have to really move toward collecting
more data about individuals to even
begin this Precision medicine process or
do we already have enough that we could
start making um some sense of of what we
already have we certainly have enough
data already to start looking at things
but it's also the case that the more
data you have the better the better job
you can do there's a tradeoff of course
between the uh the costs of the data
Collections and the benefit and I think
that one reason we have uh we kind of
went down a rabbit hole in Precision
Psychiatry is that the natural
inclination is to say psychiatrist you
know psychiatric disorders are real
illnesses and we're real doctors and
this is real biological stuff so let's
go do these biomarkers and we'll
discover
subtypes uh where we find these natural
cleavages there's not just a thing
called depression there's five
depressions and you know but it costs an
En orous amount of money to do that kind
of stuff and it hasn't really panned out
and that doesn't mean it won't
eventually but up to now we spent an
incredible amount of money on largely
underpowered studies the thinking being
that well I can't afford to do this with
thousands and thousands and thousands of
people so we'll do it with just a few
hundred and then we'll get sort of the
signal and then we'll see what's going
on it turns out statistically that if
you only have a
few hundred people as many of these
biomarker Studies have the noise that
you get in the data overwhelms the
signal and in fact you have a extremely
high probability of coming up with what
you think of as significant results that
when you then go try it on a separate
data set it totally disappears because
you're overfitting the data so so
spending incredible amounts of money on
underpowered expensive studies is not
really getting us any place and I don't
really know whether that realization has
sunk in as much as it should in the
field but we've spent an awful lot of
time in what turned out to be um not a
very productive line of Investigation so
so so this thing of getting getting
better measures is great but we can't
afford to do $10,000 specs for everybody
to you know to you know to figure out
whether a three $350 intervention is
going to be better or not just give them
the $350 intervention and see if it
works it sounds like you're saying it's
also which pieces that you pick that you
find are are relevant because if it's
just if it's too much like you said
you're overfitting but is it is part of
your work to figure out which pieces of
data are relevant and important to be
looking at well there's two pieces
there's the overfitting I mean you know
we uh we have uh in uh when you look
at claims data say and we're trying to
predict who's going to um you know have
a heart attack or who's going to drop
out of treatment or whatever it is we're
looking at we have literally thousands
and thousands of pieces of information
and if we have strong priers about
what's important we can pick a subset
and look at them but very often it's the
case that there are signals there to be
to be discovered that you wouldn't have
thought of previously and so sometimes
it makes sense to look at thousands of
pieces of evidence in genetic studies
for example that's done you know 500,000
Snips you start with but if you start
out with 500,000 things uh the chances
of you finding something that looks
significant is uh a certainty so you
know very often you have these immense
immense genetic samples and they say is
something significant at the point uh
0.01 to the minus 10th power you know
that's what you kind of look at
um the the problem there is
that you only end up getting really the
big effects so I think it is useful to
look at to look at large samples of of
predictors but you have to be thoughtful
about how you do it and so you've got to
figure out ways of doing your analysis
to minimize the chances of overfitting
and you also have to test your models in
ways that uh allow us to discover
overfitted what it exists MH and you
know it seems like for things like
cancer and heart disease maybe they've
come a long way with Precision medicine
why do you think Precision medicine has
progressed more slowly in Psychiatry
than other areas of medicine well H it
hasn't progressed all that much in heart
disease cancer it has progressed
dramatically and there are a couple of
reasons for that one big one is there's
an enormous amount of money in cancer
research and um the uh and also they
have uh they have ready access to a lot
of variables that are sort of the core
variables they need to look at okay um
the other thing is that virtually every
other branch of medicine not just cancer
but uh but the ones that haven't
progressed so far either have many fewer
treatments than we have and so that's
kind of a blessing and a curse in
psychiat
that you know what you're interested in
doing in Precision treatment is to say
here are these this range of things I
can do and is there a way of figuring

broken arms there's not really much
value in Precision medicine there's only
one thing you do when you go break your

get your arm set right but if you have

social worker a psychologist or a
private you know I mean there's all
these things and each one of those
people have a range of things they can
do so that makes it more complicated in

array of things that we can look
at but in the long run once when and if
we get organized to March through this
stuff the uh the potential value of
precision medicine it's much greater in
Psychiatry than other areas because it
helps us with th those much more
complicated decisions
uh and not just complicated decisions
but because the mechanism of action is

the possibility of getting something
that works for a person um out of that
many different things is much greater
than in other areas of medicine you know
you can you can try five or six
treatments for depression they don't
work and I'm up with a seventh and it
just boom all of a sudden it works you
you just don't have that option in place
there's only two treatments you know m
is part of this also because most of the
the way that we measure psychiatric

think that adds to the
complexity yeah uh I mean having a uh
being unclear about what the phenotype
is Muddy things certainly but you know
back to why why why are we not advancing
more than we should advance I don't
think that's a major reason and I say
that because even though it's the case

different measures of any mental

is sticking with depression if you have

cesd or the whatever you you you know if
you find a treatment that works it works
and uh the fine grain of exactly what

but I think the signal is going to be
there the real challenge is um you have

one and um uh there are many studies

it's not all that surprising that we're

are there's now enough studies most of

the uh the
specifiers that I think are most

there're probably 75 different variables
that have been found to be predictive of
differential
response and there's never been a trial
that's measured all 75 of those
variables there's never been a trial
that's measured half of those variables
um and so the big problem is we don't
have the the right predictor set and the
second big problem is that the sample
sizes that we have are too small so we
need big trials
with Rich sets of
predictors um and once we have those two
things I think we're going to find that
we can make
um progress in
Psychiatry uh equal to or greater than

do you see that is that in the work

we are um involved in uh several big

uh I think the big thing is is that we
